title: Magic Temple
html:
Hans pulls the carriage up to the temple steps and is already stepping down to open the door before I can tell him to stop. I give him my hand because everyone here does this and the alternative is a small scene I do not have the energy for. He helps me down with the practiced care of someone who has been doing this since before I arrived in this body, which he has.

The Heka temple takes up the full width of the facing block, with columns spaced wide enough that two people could walk between them side by side and still have room. The stonework is pale and very clean, which says something about the budget available to a goddess whose followers control every mana jewel in the Galahelm Empire. I straighten my posture out of habit and walk toward the entrance at the pace of someone who is exactly where she intends to be. The corset does not help. It has not helped since the moment I put it on, and nothing about the temple steps improves the situation.

The guard at the gate clocks me immediately, running a quick assessment before his face settles into something professional. He inclines his head.

"My lady. May I ask your reason for visiting today?"

"I'm here to test my mana aptitude," I tell him. "With the jewel."

He pauses. "My lady, if you have tested before. Our mana jewel has never returned an incorrect result."

"I tested before." I keep the smile in place. "I'd like to test again. If the jewel is as reliable as you say, a second test confirms what the first one found. That seems like a reasonable outcome for everyone." I pause just long enough. "Including me."

He works through it. His expression does the thing where a decision is being reached without being announced.

"The fee is 5 gold, my lady."

"Lily." She steps forward with the coin already counted. The guard accepts it with the expression of someone who has decided not to have opinions out loud, which is the correct decision. He sends for a priest and directs me to the noble waiting room.

The waiting room is off the main corridor, separated by a curtained archway and furnished with padded chairs and low tables in a style that communicates expense without specifying how much. Most of the chairs are occupied by guests in quiet conversation. I pick one near the window and sit. Nobody looks at me directly, which is fine. I cut Elena's hair down to a shorter length three days after I arrived in this body, and it still catches people off guard if they knew her before. The confusion tends to keep conversations from starting.

I look out the window at the courtyard below and wait.

The priest arrives after a few minutes, a man somewhere in his mid-thirties with the unhurried walk of someone whose position does not require speed. He greets me with a polite bow and I follow him out of the waiting room without making him ask.

The path to the testing chamber runs through a covered walkway along the inner courtyard, with painted columns and pale stone underfoot that sounds slightly different from the entrance hall. He walks it with the ease of someone who has made this particular trip a few thousand times. I do not ask questions. Lily covered the basics on the carriage ride over: Heka, goddess of magic, oldest temple in the empire, only institution with a monopoly on mana jewels. She delivered it in her calm, even voice while the city moved past the window, and I listened. What I mostly wanted was to get to the part about what to do when we arrived.

"There are quite a few different gods and goddesses in this empire," I say, mostly to keep the silence from sharpening.

"Yes, my lady. Many temples, many patrons." He glances over with something like comfortable pride. "They watch over us. It is thanks to their protection that this empire has grown so large and driven out the barbaric foreigners at its borders."

I nod at the casual racism and keep moving.

The testing chamber opens off the walkway without a door between them. Stone steps lead up to a raised platform at the center, where an orb sits on a carved pedestal with a shallow pool running around the base. Behind it stands the statue of Heka, cut from the same pale stone as the walls, long hair, hands raised with the palms facing out. Whoever made it was skilled. The face manages to look serene and authoritative at once, which is genuinely hard to do in stone.

The priest stays at the base of the steps. I go up.

I put my hand on the orb. The surface is cool and smooth, and for a moment nothing happens at all. Then something presses inward through my palm and moves up my arm in a slow, deliberate way that I cannot describe as pleasant. It reaches somewhere in my chest and spreads from there, feeling its way through whatever it finds. I keep my hand in place and my face neutral and wait for it to stop.

It fades after maybe ten seconds.

The orb is deep blue. Clear through to the other side, with a faint chill coming off the glass. A bead of condensation forms at the curve and runs slowly down to the pedestal.

Below me, the priest starts up the steps. Faster than he has moved all morning.

He stops at the top and stares at the orb. A long moment. Then he looks at me, and whatever professionalism he had arranged on his face on the way over has developed a crack in it.

"My lady has a natural gift for water magic," he says, and his voice has gone careful. "Quite rare." He looks at the orb again. Then back at me. "Congratulations."

The word comes out like he is not entirely sure what to do with it.

I keep my face polite. 'Natural gift' is a generous reading of what just happened. The jewel read a new occupant and came back with a new answer, which is either miraculous or a problem, depending on who finds out and what they make of it. I file that for later.

"I don't know very much about water magic," I say. "Would you be able to recommend some introductory reading?"

He is clearly relieved to have something useful to do. He takes me through a side corridor off the testing chamber into what functions as the temple's book supply, shelves running wall to wall with a worker at a small desk near the door. The books here are foundational, he explains. Anything above intermediate elemental theory lives in restricted collections belonging to the Blue Mage Tower and the imperial palace, neither of which I currently have access to.

"These cover water-element theory, basic spellwork, and Heka's foundational texts," he says, drawing 5 volumes from the shelf and setting them on the counter. "Together, the price is 50 gold coins."

Fifty gold is 500 silver. Real money. It also goes directly toward understanding what I apparently now have, which feeds directly into the 5-month problem. I run the arithmetic once.

"Fine. Lily has the coins. Curly orange hair, light yellow dress. One of your workers can find her in the guest room."

He sends someone immediately. While we wait, he mentions that all proceeds go to the charity fund for people in the slum district, with the warmth of someone who genuinely means it. I nod, keep the smile where it belongs, and do not mention that 50 gold would cover several months of a working family's expenses. The worker returns with a pouch of coins and the transaction closes quietly.

"Thank you for your generosity, my lady," the priest says. "May your progress in the art be swift."

I lower my head, collect the 5 books, and take my leave.

Outside, the guard from the gate is still at his post. I incline my head as I pass. He startles very slightly, catches himself, and returns the gesture with slightly more formality than necessary. I find it satisfying in a small, specific way. People here seem to expect noblewomen to look through them rather than at them, and I can never quite commit to the performance.

Hans has the carriage exactly where he left it, watching the horses with quiet attention. He opens the door before I reach it.

"Home, Hans."

I get in, stack the books on the seat beside me, and accept that the ride back will feel exactly as long as the ride over. The seat is a board with wool on top of it. I have tried two different cushion arrangements and neither one changes the fundamental situation. The road back runs through the market district and then along the outer avenue, and every joint in the paving stones announces itself.

I turn the first book over in my hands without opening it. Water magic theory, volume one.

The practical situation is this: a family that has not produced a mage in 999 years now has one. The 5-month window is still on the calendar. The title question has not moved. What has moved is the list of answers that are actually available, because some of the doors that were closed when Elena had no mana talent are no longer closed.

Which doors, how wide, and what it costs to get through any of them are things I do not know yet.

The carriage hits a rut. The books slide toward me and I put a hand out to stop them.

I open the first page.

And there, in the water-stained margin of the introduction, in a hand much older than the print, is a sentence that was not in the table of contents.

Water affinity in a bloodline is inherited or stolen. There is no third kind.
